title: Constraint-Market Compatibility: Continuing Care Retirement Communities

sections.title: Verdict Summary
sections.content:
| # | Constraint | Type | Verdict | Key reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | Emergency Escape Non-Impedance | regulatory | **none** | NFPA 101 Life Safety Code enforces identical single-motion egress principle; product design pre-satisfies |
| C2 | Mechanical Alarm Independence | regulatory | **none** | CMS CoP requires power-independent life safety; product absolutely satisfies at 0V |
| C3 | Wireless Range Limitation | physical | **mitigable** | Multi-wing campus + fire-rated memory care walls exceed 30m base range |
| C4 | IoT Battery Life Floor | operational | **mitigable** | Dementia residents test doors repeatedly; high event frequency exhausts battery below 2-yr target |
| C5 | Retrofit-Only Surface Mount | physical | **none** | Functional institutional environment; surface mount standard and expected |
| C6 | EasyWave Protocol Lock-In | operational | **mitigable** | Nurse-call panels (Stanley, Jeron) are the clinical alert destination; proprietary protocol requires GfS gateway bridge |
| C7 | IoT Price Premium | economic | **none** | Elopement liability (wrongful death, CMS penalties) >> €730/door; ROI case is overwhelming |
| C8 | GDPR Monitoring Data Compliance | regulatory | **none** | US market; GDPR inapplicable; HIPAA manageable within existing facility compliance infrastructure |
| C9 | IP Protection Boundary | environmental | **none** | Exits in heated indoor corridors/stairwells; no washdown or chemical exposure |
| C10 | EN 13637 / EltVTR Electrical Compliance | regulatory | **mitigable** | ~30% of CCRC exits use EM delayed-egress locks; combined system must maintain fail-safe release |

**Knockouts: 0 | Mitigable: 4 (C3, C4, C6, C10) | None: 6**
**Market status: SURVIVING — passes constraint screening**

---

sections.title: Mitigation Detail
sections.content:
### C3 — Wireless Range Limitation

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Why it applies** | Average CCRC = 231 IL + 34 AL + 70 SNF beds across multiple wings; memory care units use fire-rated solid walls for sound/wander control that attenuate 868 MHz; 30m base range insufficient for corridor runs |
| **Mitigation actions** | (1) RF site survey before installation to map attenuation zones; (2) deploy wireless repeaters every 30m in corridors and at floor transitions; (3) install dedicated BUS gateway per care-level cluster (MC, AL, SNF separate) |
| **Cost estimate** | medium |
| **Time estimate** | 1–2 months per wing |

### C4 — IoT Battery Life Floor

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Why it applies** | Dementia residents repeatedly test exit door handles; high door-event frequency drains 9V battery (~500 mAh) well below 2-year target; supervised access in secured MCU makes unplanned battery changes operationally constrained |
| **Mitigation actions** | (1) Enable wireless battery-status alerts to dispatch proactive replacement before depletion; (2) add quarterly battery checks to maintenance calendar aligned with facility inspection cycle; (3) specify high-capacity or low-power sleep-mode variant for memory care exits |
| **Cost estimate** | low |
| **Time estimate** | Immediate (process change); 3–6 months to establish replacement rhythm |

### C6 — EasyWave Protocol Lock-In

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Why it applies** | Nurse-call panels (Stanley Healthcare 40%, hardwired Jeron 35%) are the clinical alert destination for elopement events; EasyWave is proprietary with no native BLE/IP connectivity to these systems |
| **Mitigation actions** | (1) Deploy GfS wireless BUS gateway with potential-free contact output wired to nurse-call panel auxiliary input; (2) engage nurse-call vendor for integration wiring spec and alarm category mapping; (3) evaluate GfS IP gateway variant for direct BMS/EHR event logging |
| **Cost estimate** | medium |
| **Time estimate** | 2–4 months per care cluster |

### C10 — EN 13637 / EltVTR Electrical Compliance

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Why it applies** | ~30% of CCRC exits use electromagnetic delayed-egress locks (CMS Life Safety Code §19.2.2.2 permits 30-second delay in AL/MC); combined Türwächter + EM lock installation must maintain fail-safe escape release under all failure modes |
| **Mitigation actions** | (1) Design EM lock release to trigger on fire alarm panel signal and on power failure simultaneously with Türwächter alarm activation; (2) verify fail-safe release sequencing during commissioning with AHJ inspection; (3) document combined system as NFPA 101 §7.2.1.6 compliant |
| **Cost estimate** | low |
| **Time estimate** | 1–2 months at installation |

---

sections.title: Market-Specific Context
sections.content:
| Factor | Assessment |
|---|---|
| Primary application nodes | L6H-SAF (universal, all care levels) + L6d Memory Care (highest value — elopement prevention) |
| Current market share | 15% IoT alarm guards in CCRC (per VN L6d + L6H-SAF data); displacing mechanical-only (35%) and EM delayed-egress (30%) |
| Key differentiator | Power-independent alarm satisfies CMS CoP Life Safety simultaneously with elopement prevention — no alternative delivers both in a single retrofit device |
| Dominant mitigation cluster | C3 + C4 + C6 form the "CCRC readiness package"; deploying all three resolves the market's operational requirements |
| Mitigation complexity | **Medium** — 4 mitigations required, none blocking; all solvable within standard deployment budget |

---

assessments.rationale: NFPA 101 Life Safety Code imposes the identical single-motion egress principle as EN 179/EN 1125. Product's mechanical design inherently satisfies the underlying requirement; no friction with CCRC regulatory environment.

CMS Conditions of Participation and NFPA Life Safety Code require life-safety systems to operate under power failure. Product's mechanical-primary architecture operates at 0V — this constraint is pre-satisfied and reinforces the product's value proposition.

mitigation_actions: RF site survey to map signal attenuation zones across campus wings
Deploy wireless repeaters every 30m in corridors and at floor transitions
Install dedicated BUS gateway per care-level cluster (MC, AL, SNF)
assessments.rationale: Average CCRC campus spans multiple wings with fire-rated construction in memory care units. 30m base range insufficient for long corridor runs; repeater mesh and per-cluster gateways provide full coverage — standard mitigation with no engineering novelty.

mitigation_actions: Enable wireless battery-status alerts for proactive replacement dispatch
Add quarterly battery checks to facility maintenance calendar
Specify high-capacity or low-power variant for memory care exits
assessments.mitigation_time_estimate: Immediate (process change); 3–6 months to establish rhythm
assessments.rationale: Dementia residents frequently test exit doors, creating high event frequency that exhausts 9V battery below the 2-year target. Supervised-access requirement in secured MCUs makes unplanned battery replacement operationally difficult. Battery-status alerts and scheduled maintenance cycles fully mitigate.
CCRC environments prioritize functional safety over architectural aesthetics. Surface-mounted safety hardware is standard and expected in institutional care settings across all care levels.

mitigation_actions: Deploy GfS wireless BUS gateway with potential-free contact output to nurse-call panel auxiliary input
Engage nurse-call vendor (Stanley Healthcare, Jeron) for integration wiring spec and alarm category mapping
Evaluate GfS IP gateway for direct BMS/EHR event logging if required
assessments.rationale: CCRC nurse-call panels (Stanley Healthcare 40%, hardwired Jeron 35%) are the clinical destination for elopement alerts. EasyWave's proprietary protocol requires a GfS BUS gateway with relay output to bridge into nurse-call — adds hardware and integration effort per cluster but is architecturally straightforward.

Elopement liability exposure (wrongful death suits, CMS penalties, increased staffing) far exceeds the €730/door premium. Professional B2B procurement in regulated care is ROI-driven; the liability case is unambiguous. Cost is never a knockout.

CCRCs are US-based institutions (~2,000 in US); GDPR does not apply. Door-event data may implicate HIPAA PHI rules under patient location data interpretation, but falls within existing HIPAA compliance infrastructure — no new regulatory framework required.

CCRC emergency exits are in heated indoor corridors, stairwells, and ground-floor vestibule doors. No washdown, chemical splash, or extreme humidity exposure. Standard IP class is sufficient for all deployment locations.

mitigation_actions: Design EM lock to release on fire alarm signal and on power failure simultaneously with Türwächter alarm activation
Verify fail-safe release sequencing during commissioning with AHJ inspection
Document combined system compliance with NFPA 101 §7.2.1.6
assessments.rationale: ~30% of CCRC exits use electromagnetic delayed-egress locks (CMS §19.2.2.2 permits 30-sec delay in AL/MC). Combined Türwächter + EM lock systems must maintain fail-safe escape release. System-level design compliance is well-understood; commissioning test and AHJ acceptance are standard.
